In general terms, the introduction states the intention, while the conclusion defines the\nachievement of an essay.  Together they constitute the frame for your paper, providing the first and last\nopportunities to convince your reader of its value.  Because they occupy such rhetorically important\npositions in the structure of your paper, the introduction and conclusion should be crafted with care.\n\nThere is no single formula for writing introductions and conclusions.  The strategy you adopt will\ndepend on the nature of the assignment, the conventions of the academic discipline, and the internal\nimperatives of your particular argument. And they help your reader understand the value of your argument.\n\nTHE INTRODUCTION\n\nThe introduction typically serves several purposes:\n\n(cid:121)  It sets the tone for your paper.\n\nRemember that when you write the introduction, you are introducing not only your\nargument, but also yourself, the author.  What kind of persona do you want to project?\nPresumably, you do not want to come across as dogmatic, impatient, or hostile toward your\nsubject or others who have written about it.  This kind of stance will likely alienate a reader,\nwho may choose simply to stop reading.  In academic writing, it is much more effective to\npresent yourself as a thoughtful writer who has a good command of the subject, who can\nacknowledge complexity and nuance, who has made reasonable judgments on the basis of\ntextual or factual evidence, and who is aware of alternative interpretations.  In any case, the\ntone of the introduction is an important part of any strategy of persuasion.\n\n(cid:121)  It identifies and limits your subject.\n\nYour introduction tells a reader what you are writing about and defines the scope of your\ninquiry.\n\n(cid:121)  It orients your reader by explaining the specific context and rationale for your\n\nargument.\nYour reader looks to the introduction not only for the thesis statement or central idea, but\nalso for orientation within the chosen area of inquiry.  Take care, therefore, to provide any\nbackground or context that will help your reader understand how your work fits into the\nlarger picture, and why it matters.  Locate your work on the conceptual map.\n\n(cid:121)  It presents your thesis or central claim about your subject.\n\nThere is no absolute rule on the length and placement of the thesis statement.  But a good\nrule of thumb for a short (5-page) paper is to state the thesis in one or two sentences at the\nend of the first paragraph.\n\n\x0c(cid:121)  It engages your reader\'s attention and persuades her to keep reading.\n\nYour introduction should present an original thesis on a significant topic within a well-\ndefined subject area.  But there are different ways of accomplishing this in an engaging way.\nFor example, you might approach your topic by\n\n(cid:121)  moving from the specific to the general: use an anecdote, quotation, fact, or textual\n\ndetail that illustrates the larger issues\n\n(cid:121)  moving from the general to the specific: first present a somewhat wider view* of the\n\nsubject, then focus in on the particular area of inquiry.\n\n*Take care, however, that the “wide view” does not become an\noccasion for windy generality and vague abstraction.  The wide\nview should itself be focused, concrete, and directly relevant to\nyour particular topic.   [See below, “Formulaic Introductions”]\n\n(cid:121)  posing a significant question or problem that serves to focus your inquiry\n\n(cid:121)  challenging a commonplace interpretation\n\n(cid:121)  Sometimes, especially in longer essays, the introduction describes the structure of your\n\nessay.\nA brief overview of the organization of your paper can help prepare your reader to follow\nyour argument.\n\nThe Challenge of Writing the Introduction\n\nWriting the introduction can be a daunting task, and it is natural to feel anxious about it.  After all, you\ncan write a good introduction only if you have a clearly defined thesis statement and a sure command of\nthe relevant evidence.  Since these things will emerge only after patient grappling with your texts, it\nhelps if you first prepare to write your introduction by studying your materials, taking notes, gathering\nevidence, organizing ideas, and proposing a possible line of argument.  Doing this preparatory work will\nmake the task of writing the introduction much easier.  Then you can revisit it as the paper continues to\ntake shape.  Some tips:\n\nBe prepared to revise your introduction at any point during the writing process.\nIn the course of drafting the essay you may discover a more compelling argument than the one with\nwhich you began. It establishes the relevance and background of the research, outlines what the study will address, and engages the reader\'s interest. By effectively presenting the problem at hand, it cultivates a clear expectation and prepares readers for what follows. This engagement is key for enhancing retention as a strong introduction positively influences how much information a reader deems important and memorable.\\n\\nConversely, the conclusion is equally critical in reinforcing reader retention. It encapsulates the core findings and implications of the study, allowing readers to reflect on the significance of what they have read. A well-crafted conclusion summarizes key insights while also hinting at future implications or areas for further research, thereby enhancing the likelihood of retention. Despite its benefits, ER remains underutilized in many EFL contexts. This study investigates the impact of ER on the academic writing skills of 80 undergraduate students at Mizan Tepi University, selected through convenience sampling. Data were gathered through pre- and post-training comprehension tests, structured questionnaires on reading strategy preferences, essay analysis, and instructor interviews. A four-week ER program was implemented, engaging students with literary texts including short stories, novels, and poems to foster independent reading and comprehension. Quantitative data were analyzed using independent and paired sample t-tests and descriptive statistics. Results showed a moderate and statistically significant positive correlation between ER engagement and writing proficiency (r = 0.57, p &lt; 0.001), with notable improvements in lexical diversity and argumentative coherence. Essay scores increased substantially: third-year students improved from 57.1 to 74.9, and fourth-year students from 59.5 to 78.5. Qualitative feedback from instructors highlighted ER’s role in enhancing metacognitive awareness, facilitating the transfer of implicit to explicit knowledge, and improving overall coherence in student writing. However, challenges such as limited access to graded readers, weak institutional support, and inconsistent student motivation constrained its full effectiveness. This study underlines ER’s dual function as a linguistic resource and cognitive scaffold. Despite its benefits, ER remains underutilized in many EFL contexts. This study investigates the impact of ER on the academic writing skills of 80 undergraduate students at Mizan Tepi University, selected through convenience sampling. Data were gathered through pre- and post-training comprehension tests, structured questionnaires on reading strategy preferences, essay analysis, and instructor interviews. A four-week ER program was implemented, engaging students with literary texts including short stories, novels, and poems to foster independent reading and comprehension. Quantitative data were analyzed using independent and paired sample t-tests and descriptive statistics. Results showed a moderate and statistically significant positive correlation between ER engagement and writing proficiency (r = 0.57, p &lt; 0.001), with notable improvements in lexical diversity and argumentative coherence. Essay scores increased substantially: third-year students improved from 57.1 to 74.9, and fourth-year students from 59.5 to 78.5. Qualitative feedback from instructors highlighted ER’s role in enhancing metacognitive awareness, facilitating the transfer of implicit to explicit knowledge, and improving overall coherence in student writing. However, challenges such as limited access to graded readers, weak institutional support, and inconsistent student motivation constrained its full effectiveness. This study underlines ER’s dual function as a linguistic resource and cognitive scaffold.
